title: An Invariance Principle of 1D KPZ with Robin Boundary Conditions
abstract: We consider a discrete one-dimensional random interface on the half-space whose height at any positive point is composed of a function of the heights at its two closest neighbours and an independent random noise background. In [AC24], Adhikari and Chatterjee proved for the full-space model that the height function of such a Markov process converges to the Cole-Hopf solution of the 1D KPZ equation under a parabolic rescaling as the variance of the noise variables goes to zero in the intermediate disorder regime, assuming the dependency of neighboring heights is equivariant, symmetric, and at least six times differentiable in a neighborhood of zero. In this paper, we obtained the same convergence result for the half-space model with a Neumann boundary condition.
extracted_text:
An Invariance Principle for the 1D KPZ Equation with
Neumann Boundary Conditions
Yiming Tang1
1Department of Mathematics, University of Toronto
June 2025
Abstract
We consider a discrete one-dimensional random interface on the half-space whose height at any
positive point is composed of a function of the heights at its two closest neighbours and an inde-
pendent random noise background. In [AC24], Adhikari and Chatterjee proved for the full-space
model that the height function of such a Markov process converges to the Cole-Hopf solution of the
1D KPZ equation under a parabolic rescaling as the variance of the noise variables goes to zero in
the intermediate disorder regime, assuming the dependency of neighboring heights is equivariant,
symmetric, and at least six times differentiable in a neighborhood of zero. In this paper, we obtained
the same convergence result for the half-space model with a Neumann boundary condition.
1
Introduction
The 1-dimensional KPZ equation
∂th(x, t) = (∂xh(x, t))2 + ∂2
xh(x, t) + ξ,
(1)
is a two-dimensional stochastic partial differential equation introduced by Kardar, Parisi, and Zhang
[KPZ86] to model a stochastic interface growth process, with ξ being the space-time white noise. In
relations with other SPDEs, its partial derivative ∂xh(x, t) solves the stochastic Burgers equation and
exp(h(x, t)) is a mild solution of the stochastic heat equation (SHE) through the Cole-Hopf transforma-
tion. The latter was made precise through regularity structures [Hai14]. The KPZ equation, as one of
the most important models in the KPZ universality class [Wu25], is also a scaling limit of many weakly
asymmetric interface growth models; this is known as weak KPZ universality. In particular, we focus
on half-space models which involve interactions with a boundary. Previous works, including but not
limited to the ones cited, have shown the weak universality result in the half space for directed polymers
[Wu20] [Par22] [BC23], asymmetric exclusion processes [CS18] [Par19], and stochastic six-vertex models
[BBCW18] under various boundary conditions.
In the recent work [AC24], Adhikari and Chatterjee proved the weak KPZ universality conjecture
for a general type of 1D KPZ equation in the absence of a stationary measure. It is natural for us
to ask if we have the same weak universality result in the half-space. The growing interface could be
either rewarded or penalized at the boundary. In the main result Theorem 2.1, we will show that the
height function converges to the log of the mild solution of the SHE with Robin boundary conditions
and multiplicative space-time white noise stated as follow
∂tZ(x, t) = 1
2∂2
xZ(x, t) +
p
2µ2βZ(x, t)ξ,
∂xZ(0, t) = AZ(0, t).
Here, A, ν2 and β are deterministic constant parameters of the SHE and ξ again is the space-time white
noise. We will specify how ν2 and β are determined in the next section as we explicitly define our general
type of half-space 1D KPZ equation model.
2
Main result
Let us denote the background noise in the non-negative half-space by a set of i.i.d. random variables
by (y(x, t))x∈N0,t∈N0, where N0 is all natural numbers including 0. We assume y(x, t) has a mean zero

ψ is at least six times continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of the origin.

Here, γN is the reflection rate at the boundary x = 0. The boundary is absorbing if 0 < γN < 1,
totally reflecting if γN = 1, or rewarding if γN > 1. fN(x, t) denotes the height of the surface at location
x at time t. (2) means that this height is a function of the heights at location x −1 and x + 1 at time
t −1, when away from the boundary, plus a random noise decaying with N.
Theorem 2.1. With m being the moment generating function of the noise variable y(x, t), consider a
rescaled and renormalized surface growth process efN : N −1/2N0 × N −1N0 →R defined by
efN(x, t) := fN(

Then extend efN to (R≥0)2 via a linear interpolation. Assuming the initial condition converges fast
enough (to be specified in Section 3), exp(β efN) converges in law to a solution Z of the SHE on the
half space with Robin boundary conditions and multiplicative space-time white noise, with respect to the
topology of uniform convergence on compact sets on C((R≥0)2).
Remark 2.2. For x ∈Z and t ∈N0, let p(x, t) denote the probability that a simple symmetric random
walk on Z starting from 0 at time 0, is at x at time t. Define ∆(x, t) := p(x + 1, t) −p(x −1, t) and c :=

result in [AC24]. We will justify the constant in Section 8.
Remark 2.3. The idea follows from the same approach as [AC24], through a comparison between the
proposed model and the half-space directed polymer model which was known to converge to the Cole-Hopf
solution of the half-space KPZ equation with Neumann boundary conditions from [Wu20]. The major
difficulty of our work was to verify if the various bounds in [AC24] remain valid in the presence of a
reflection parameter and in a lack of complete independence of the noise background when we later mirror
the half-space noise variables to the full space. Another difference is that, in Section 8, we are able to
prove precisely that the decorrelation is fast through continuous approximations and then we conclude the
same result as in [AC24] in the half-space setting; that is, under the stated assumptions, fN(x, t) under
parabolic scaling of space and time converges in law to a limit that differs from the Cole-Hopf solution of
the 1D KPZ equation on the half space with Neumann boundary condition by a deterministic linear term
in t.
Remark 2.4. The three conditions we impose on ψ are the same as the full-space model defined in
[AC24]. Let us give a brief reasoning on why such assumptions are natural. For weak KPZ universality,
we consider models such that the interface growth is driven by lateral growth, certain smoothing mecha-
nisms, and random forcing. Such a model should only care about the relative heights with its neighbors
(not necessarily nearest) instead of any absolute heights, and thus condition (a) is reasonable to assume.
Condition (b) is assumed for simplicity of the proof. In condition (c), the first two derivatives are essen-
tial as the directed polymer model we wish to compare to must have an inverse temperature β = ∂2
1ψ(0, 0).
That we further require ψ ∈C6 is purely because the proof technique relies on expressing the recursion
via Taylor expansions and controlling the leading terms.
2


2.1
Notations
By considering ψ0(u, v) := ψ(u, v) −ψ(0, 0) and combining with the equivariance property of ψ as in
[AC24], we can safely assume ψ(0, 0) = 0 without loss of generality.
From now on, we will fix N and drop the N subscript for the simplicity of writing. We will use
f : N0 × N0 →R to denote the function f(x, t) := fN(x, t) −tβ−1 log m(N −1/4β). Let us also define a
function ϕ : R →R by ϕ(u) = ψ

in a neighborhood of zero.
We will also use big "O" and small "o" notations in the rest of the paper. We say A(x, t) = O(N k)
if there exists some constant C which does not dependent on N, x or t such that |A(x, t)|≤CN k for all
N large enough. We will specify what C might depend on in the text, as it varies case by case. Similarly,
we say A(x, t) = o(N k) if |A(x, t)|/N k converges to 0 as N →∞uniformly in the given region of (x, t).

both mathematical contents and writing advice. We would also like to thank the anonymous referees for
their helpful comments.

The Main Argument
In this section, we will adapt the most important step in proving the result of [AC24] to our half-space
model, which estimates the difference between the height function of a directed polymer model and a
general height function f as defined in (2). Recall the constant c defined in Remark 2.2 and fix some
sufficiently small ϵ > 0, define the "renormalization term"
Y (x, t) := 16c

Theorem 7.1. Fix a, b > 0 and a sufficiently small ϵ > 0. There is an event Ω2 with P (Ω2) = 1 −o(1),
such that on Ω2, we have that for all (x, t) ∈[0, aN] × [0, bN],
f(x, t) = f poly (x, t) + Y (x, t) + o(1)
where the o(1) term is uniform in (x, t).
Proof. It is easy to check that the same result in Lemma 8.1 in [AC24] follows and we define ΩY to
be the event where for all (x, t) ∈[1, a

f(x, t) −f poly (x, t) −Y (x, t).
The induction argument from Theorem 8.2 in [AC24] follows when we are away from the boundary.
So it remains to prove that for all (x, t) ∈D := {(x, t) ∈N0 × N0 : x + t ≤min{aN, bN}}, having
|δ(x, s)|≤N −1−ϵ/2s for all s ≤t −1 implies |δ(0, t −1)|≤N −1−ϵ/2t for any N > N0, where N0 is
deterministic.
We will work under the assumption that Ω2 holds. Note that δ(x, 0) = 0 for all x. Fix (x, t) ∈D.
By definition, notice that Y (0, t) = Y (1, t −1) + 16c
β4 K(0, t)4. Substitute it into the formula of δ(x, t) to
get
|δ(0, t)|= |δ(1, t −1) −16cβ−4K(0, t)4|≤|δ(1, t −1)|+|16cβ−4K(0, t)4|.
(35)
From the definition of K, as ∆(z, s)ξ(|z|, s) is an odd function over z, K(0, t) = 0. Hence, we may
conclude that |δ(0, t)|≤|δ(1, t −1)|≤N −1−ϵ/2t.

Concentration of the Renormalization Term
In this section, we will prove that Y (x, t) asymptotically behaves like a constant multiple of t, and will
evaluate that constant.
Lemma 8.1. Fix a, b > 0 and any sufficiently small ϵ > 0. There is an event ΩY LLN with P (ΩY LLN) =
1 −o(1), such that on ΩY LLN, we have that for all (x, t) ∈(0, aN] × [0, bN], |Y (x, t) −E(Y (x, t))|= o(1)
where the o(1) term is uniform in (x, t).
The proof of Lemma 8.1 follows exactly the same from the argument as in Lemma 9.1 in [AC24].
